deck.title: Auto & Shop — Hand Tools
deck.description: Identify the tool by what it's used for. The ASVAB AS subtest leans on tool recognition.

cards.front: Used to drive nails into wood and pry them back out
cards.explanation: The curved claw on the back is for pulling nails; a framing hammer looks similar but has a straighter, longer claw for rough construction.

cards.front: Used to drive screws with a single straight-slot head
cards.explanation: Also called a flat-blade or standard screwdriver; distinct from Phillips, Torx, or hex drivers because the tip is a flat blade.

cards.front: Used to measure length by pulling out a flexible metal blade
cards.explanation: The hooked end is intentionally loose so it reads accurately whether you push it against a surface or hook it over an edge.

cards.front: Used to drive screws with a cross-shaped recess in the head
cards.explanation: Designed to cam out under high torque to prevent overtightening; not interchangeable with Pozidriv or square-drive bits.

cards.front: Used with interchangeable cylindrical heads to turn nuts and bolts quickly
cards.explanation: The ratcheting mechanism lets you turn a fastener without removing and repositioning the tool between strokes.

cards.front: Used to grip and bend wire or reach into tight spaces with thin tapered jaws
cards.explanation: Ideal for electrical work and small parts; differs from lineman's pliers, which have flat gripping jaws and a side cutter.

cards.front: Used to determine whether a surface is perfectly horizontal or vertical
cards.explanation: A bubble centered between the lines on the vial indicates true level or plumb depending on the vial's orientation.

cards.front: Used to cut metal pipe, rod, or bar stock with a thin replaceable blade in a frame
cards.explanation: The blade is tensioned in a C-shaped frame and is installed with the teeth pointing forward so it cuts on the push stroke.

cards.front: Used to mark and check 90-degree corners when laying out wood
cards.explanation: The sliding head also checks 45-degree miters and includes a small bubble level and a scribe, unlike a fixed try square.

cards.front: Used to drive recessed fasteners with a six-sided star-shaped tip
cards.explanation: Often called an Allen wrench or hex key; the L-shape lets you switch between fast turning and high torque by flipping it.

cards.front: Used to grip large nuts or pipes by sliding the lower jaw along an angled track
cards.explanation: The tongue-and-groove channels let you set a wide jaw opening that won't slip closed under load, unlike basic slip-joint pliers.

cards.front: Used to shape metal by striking with a rounded face on one end and a hemispherical face on the other
cards.explanation: The rounded peen is used for peening rivets and shaping metal; a claw hammer's softer steel would deform if struck on metal stock.

cards.front: Used to measure inside, outside, and depth dimensions to about a thousandth of an inch
cards.explanation: More versatile than a micrometer because of the four measuring surfaces, but typically less precise than a micrometer at the same range.

cards.front: Used to tighten a fastener to a precise, specified amount of rotational force
cards.explanation: A click-type wrench releases or signals when the set torque is reached; critical for lug nuts, head bolts, and other engineered joints.

cards.front: Used to grip irregular objects by clamping with adjustable, locking jaws
cards.explanation: An over-center toggle holds the jaws shut hands-free, unlike standard pliers that rely on continuous hand pressure.

cards.front: Used to grip and turn round pipe by digging serrated jaws into the pipe wall
cards.explanation: The angled, toothed jaws bite harder as you pull, leaving marks; a smooth-jawed adjustable wrench would slip on round pipe.

cards.front: Used to deliver heavy blows without bouncing back or marring the workpiece
cards.explanation: The hollow head is filled with shot or sand that absorbs the rebound; a rubber mallet is similar but lighter and bouncier.

cards.front: Used to measure outside dimensions to a thousandth of an inch using a calibrated thimble and spindle
cards.explanation: Reads to 0.001" on a standard mechanical micrometer (vernier scale extends to 0.0001"); limited to about a one-inch range per tool, so machinists own a set.

cards.front: Used to drive a stuck pin out of a hole after a starter punch has loosened it
cards.explanation: Has a long straight shank of constant diameter so it can pass deep into the hole; a center punch has a tapered tip for marking, not driving.

cards.front: Used to cut wood along the grain with chisel-like teeth that act like tiny gouges
cards.explanation: Rip-saw teeth are filed straight across to chisel out fibers along the grain; a crosscut saw has knife-like beveled teeth that sever fibers across the grain.
